posts.cooked:
Opened for the sake of science, i.e. “What does really traditionally styled Cote Rotie taste like brutally young?”

Answer: poor, tired, nasty, brutish and short, as Hobbes might put it. Has a stemmy, cedar note, and a profound astringency that is all but insurmountable and which obliterates nearly all else. Savory Syrah notes underneath and a dollop of black fruit struggling to peel through the barbed wire tannic fence. This is currently a wine for masochists and will likely be so for a decade or more. Drink with caution.

Edit: 2013 vintage

posts.name: alan snitow
posts.cooked: vintage?

posts.name: Markus S
posts.cooked: Noah, do you have a year for this?

posts.name: R0bërt Gilb@ne
posts.cooked: Did you finish it off?

posts.name: Matt Latuchie
posts.cooked: if you have extras, i’d be happy to buy them.

posts.name: Gene Ferrara
posts.cooked:
I had an 11 recently solely b\c I suspected the bottle was damaged.

My note:

Suspected leaker, bottle less than 1" ullage, cork looked perfect.

VG+. Typical CR/Levet - meaty, bloody, olive herbal vg and no sign of damage.

With grilled chicken, dark and brooding, closing up, bitter but not in an unpleasant way, more like almonds but not showing much else. Young, tannic

JMHO, they are the typical 15 year from vintage date CR wines.

I’ve had the 06 and 07 in the last few years and they were tight and harsh.

The first couple of sips you get a hint of what the future greatness may be, then the structure starts to close it down.
Still sitting on all my bottles. The 2010’s showed well close to release. However, these are wines for the patient, deep, cold cellars. Check in around 20 years…maybe.

posts.name: Markus S
posts.cooked: Well that explains it. I suspect these will need the classic Northern Rhone (except St. Joe) guideline of not touching until their 15th b’day…well, okay, sometimes some are good at 12+, but the really structured/acidic vintages need their time in bottle. Hands off those other ones!

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked: In June the back half of a bottle of 2010 drank extraordinarily well on the third day it was open. I’d guess the 2013 would open up some with extended airing.

In September of 2014, we had a dinner focused on Levet. We opened ten vintages of Chavaroche ranging from 2011 to 1998. Keith had a good write up, which might provide some guidance on how these develop over time:

https://www.cellartracker.com/m/stories/26092

link_counts.title: Rotating Dinner Series- Bernard Levet Cote-Roties - CellarTracker

posts.name: Keith Levenberg
posts.cooked:
The 2013 Chavaroche is not drinkable now. The 2013 Journaries is drinking spectacularly well now. I don’t know why this is. Wine moves in mysterious ways.

Once Levets close down, they stay there for a long time. I had a 1999 a few weeks ago that was just entering its window of peak drinkability - it was totally shut down 2 or 3 years ago.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: The '06s are youthfully approachable now too. Of course they’ll be better in a decade, but showing a lot already with a decant.

posts.name: Adam Noble
posts.cooked: One of the things I have learned on this board over the years is that Keith L is never wrong about Northern Rhone (or anything else I can think of).

posts.name: Chris V a n d e r m a r e l
posts.cooked: I tasted through a couple 2013s with Agnes Levet in Cote Rotie in July. I had never had Levet before. Based on reputation I was expecting something very savoury (olive, smoke, meat, pepper), but found the wines very fruit forward. I struggled to determine whether this change had to do with the winemaking change from Bernard to Agnes or whether the wines just need age. Hoping it’s the latter as I bought some.
posts.display_username: Chris V a n d e r m a r e l

posts.name: Martin Steinley
posts.cooked: I sell a lot of Levet, so I drink it with some regularity. IMO, going back to 2004, the best vintage to drink now is the 2008 (and possibly the 2007 Journaries). Given what the Levet family packs in the bottle, there is nothing wrong with a little less horsepower. The 2005 Chavaroche I had recently was a beast of a wine. Rosenthal held it back longer than other vintages, but an argument could be made that he should still be holding it.

posts.name: Don Cornutt
posts.cooked:
Keith Levenberg:

The 2013 Chavaroche is not drinkable now. The 2013 Journaries is drinking spectacularly well now. I don’t know why this is. Wine moves in mysterious ways.

Once Levets close down, they stay there for a long time. I had a 1999 a few weeks ago that was just entering its window of peak drinkability - it was totally shut down 2 or 3 years ago.

Thanks. My 99 Chavaroches have been pretty tight for some time. Glad to hear it might be opening.

Martin Steinley:

I sell a lot of Levet, so I drink it with some regularity. IMO, going back to 2004, the best vintage to drink now is the 2008 (and possibly the 2007 Journaries). Given what the Levet family packs in the bottle, there is nothing wrong with a little less horsepower. The 2005 Chavaroche I had recently was a beast of a wine. Rosenthal held it back longer than other vintages, but an argument could be made that he should still be holding it.

Agreed on the '05. I had one back in the early fall and it was bigger than normal (but outstanding). I found it a lot more open than one I’d had a year or two before that, though still with plenty of tannin and bite.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Chris V.:

I tasted through a couple 2013s with Agnes Levet in Cote Rotie in July. I had never had Levet before. Based on reputation I was expecting something very savoury (olive, smoke, meat, pepper), but found the wines very fruit forward. I struggled to determine whether this change had to do with the winemaking change from Bernard to Agnes or whether the wines just need age. Hoping it’s the latter as I bought some.

Agnès has been making the wines for quite a while now, more than a decade I think.

You must have just visited on a fruit day or something.
